content.title: Action Potential
content.definition: A rapid, transient reversal and restoration of membrane potential driven by sequential opening and closing of voltage-gated ion channels, propagating signals along excitable cells.
content.summary: Action potentials are the fundamental unit of electrical signaling in neurons and muscle. The same biophysical mechanism is studied in biology, neuroscience, and clinical electrophysiology.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Explains how excitable cells generate and propagate electrical signals.
Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.
Basis for ECG, arrhythmia mechanisms, and local anesthetic targets.
Explains how excitable cells generate and propagate electrical signals.

content.title: Action Potential Phases
content.definition: The sequence of depolarization, repolarization, and afterhyperpolarization in excitable cells associated with membrane potential overshoot and return to resting state.
content.summary: Depolarization follows rapid Na⁺ entry; repolarization results from Na⁺ inactivation and K⁺ efflux. Afterhyperpolarization briefly hyperpolarizes the membrane below resting potential.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Links ion channel kinetics to the characteristic voltage trace of excitable cells.
Contrast with cardiac AP; basis for neural pharmacology.
Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

content.title: Threshold and All-or-None Response
content.definition: The membrane potential at which depolarization becomes self-sustaining and triggers a maximal action potential, with subthreshold stimuli producing graded but non-propagating responses.
content.summary: Excitable cells integrate subthreshold inputs until threshold is reached, then fire an all-or-none action potential that propagates without decrement.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Explains how neurons convert graded inputs into digital signals.
EPSP/IPSP summation and firing threshold on Step 1.
Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

content.title: Axonal Propagation and Conduction
content.definition: Regenerative spread of action potentials along axons, accelerated by myelination and saltatory conduction at nodes.
content.summary: Local current loops depolarize adjacent membrane; myelin reduces capacitance and metabolic load.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

content.title: Cardiac Action Potential Overview (Non-Pacemaker)
content.definition: A comparative overview of ventricular myocyte action potentials highlighting prolonged depolarization from calcium influx creating a plateau phase, distinct from brief neuronal action potentials.
content.summary: Cardiac myocytes have a longer action potential due to L-type calcium channel-mediated plateau. Detailed phase 0–4 ionic currents belong under cardiac membrane potential L3; this node establishes contrast only.

framing.relevance: Comparative only — phase detail and antiarrhythmic mechanisms live in dedicated L3 nodes.

existing_anchor_concepts._reviewer_note: Do not add L5 antiarrhythmic detail here — forward reference to antiarrhythmic_drug_classes L3.

content.title: Local Anesthetic Mechanism (Na⁺ Channel Blockade)
content.definition: Use-dependent blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels by local anesthetic agents, preventing depolarization and action potential propagation in sensory neurons.
content.summary: Local anesthetics bind sodium channels from the intracellular side preferentially in open or inactivated states, blocking propagation in high-frequency firing fibers first.

framing.relevance: Links AP physiology to pharmacology — use dependence and pKa.

content.title: Absolute and Relative Refractory Periods
content.definition: Intervals following an action potential during which a second stimulus cannot (absolute) or only with greater strength (relative) elicit another action potential due to Na⁺ channel inactivation and K⁺ channel activity.
content.summary: The absolute refractory period enforces unidirectional propagation. The relative refractory period limits maximal firing frequency when stimuli arrive in rapid succession.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Explains firing frequency limits and directionality of signal propagation.
Limits maximal firing frequency; explains directionality of AP propagation.
Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

existing_anchor_concepts._reviewer_note: MERGED from 3 contexts: biology:spine_biology_l4_refractory_periods, psychology_neuroscience:spine_psychology_neuroscience_l5_refractory_periods, medicine_preclinical:spine_medicine_preclinical_l5_refractory_periods | DEPTH RECONCILED: members authored at mixed L4/L5; promoted to L4 so shallow contexts can include it. Confirm L4 vs L5.

content.title: Continuous and Saltatory Conduction
content.definition: Propagation of action potentials along unmyelinated axons by sequential depolarization of adjacent membrane regions, or along myelinated axons by jumping between nodes of Ranvier.
content.summary: Myelin increases conduction velocity by insulating internodal regions and concentrating current at nodes. Saltatory conduction is energetically efficient for long-distance signaling.

framing.relevance: How action potentials travel along axons to reach synaptic terminals.

content.title: Voltage-Gated Na⁺ and K⁺ Channel Sequence
content.definition: The ordered opening and inactivation of voltage-gated sodium channels followed by delayed opening of voltage-gated potassium channels that produce depolarization and repolarization of excitable membranes.
content.summary: Sodium influx drives rapid depolarization; sodium inactivation and potassium efflux restore negative membrane potential and define action potential shape.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.title_in_context: Voltage-Gated Na⁺ and K⁺ Channel Sequence
framing.relevance: Mechanistic basis for action potential generation in neurons and muscle.

framing.title_in_context: Voltage-Gated Na⁺ and K⁺ Channel Sequence
framing.relevance: Mechanistic basis for local anesthetics and antiarrhythmics targeting Na⁺ channels.

framing.title_in_context: Voltage-Gated Na⁺ and K⁺ Channel Sequence
framing.relevance: Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

content.title: Saltatory Conduction in Myelinated Axons
content.definition: Rapid action potential propagation in myelinated axons where depolarization jumps between nodes of Ranvier, regenerating at each node while myelin insulates internodal segments.
content.summary: Saltatory conduction increases conduction velocity and metabolic efficiency. Demyelination disrupts this mechanism and slows or blocks signal transmission.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Also used in psychology_neuroscience (neural signaling) and medicine_preclinical (cardiac/neural electrophysiology).

framing.relevance: Explains MS demyelination conduction failure.
Foundation of information coding and transmission in the nervous system.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Adaptive Immunity Overview
content.definition: Antigen-specific lymphocyte responses with clonal selection generating immunologic memory and heightened secondary responses to previously encountered pathogens.
content.summary: Adaptive immunity combines humoral and cell-mediated arms. Memory B and T cells underpin vaccination and faster recall responses.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged duplicate adaptive_immunity_overview nodes in biology and medicine_preclinical.

framing.relevance: Specific, memory-capable immune layer in vertebrates.
Framework for immunodeficiency, vaccination, and transplant rejection.
Specific, memory-capable immune layer in vertebrates.

content.title: Clonal Selection and Lymphocyte Expansion
content.definition: Process by which antigen binding to a lymphocyte receptor selects that clone for proliferation and differentiation, producing effector cells and memory cells specific to the encountered antigen.
content.summary: Clonal selection explains antigen specificity and memory in adaptive immunity. Each lymphocyte recognizes one epitope; antigen encounter drives clonal expansion.

framing.relevance: Core mechanism linking antigen recognition to specific immune responses.

content.title: Clonal Selection and Affinity Maturation
content.definition: Antigen-driven proliferation and differentiation of lymphocytes bearing antigen-specific receptors, with somatic hypermutation refining antibody affinity in germinal centers.
content.summary: Clonal selection explains how a vast receptor repertoire yields a focused response. Affinity maturation improves antibody quality during secondary responses.

framing.relevance: Foundation for understanding lymphoma and immunodeficiency.

content.title: Humoral and Cell-Mediated Immunity
content.definition: Two adaptive arms in which B lymphocytes produce circulating antibodies (humoral) and T lymphocytes directly kill infected cells or coordinate responses (cell-mediated).
content.summary: Humoral immunity targets extracellular pathogens via antibody neutralization and opsonization. Cell-mediated immunity eliminates intracellular pathogens through cytotoxic T cells and helper T cell coordination.

framing.relevance: Framework for understanding how adaptive immunity addresses diverse pathogen niches.
Framework for immunodeficiency classification.

content.title: Immunologic Memory Mechanisms
content.definition: Persistence of long-lived memory B and T cells with altered activation thresholds enabling rapid recall responses upon re-exposure to the same antigen.
content.summary: Memory lymphocytes circulate and reside in lymphoid and peripheral tissues. They respond faster and with greater magnitude than naïve cells.

framing.relevance: Basis for vaccine durability and serologic testing.
applications: Central vs effector memory T cells

content.title: Immunological Memory Cells
content.definition: Long-lived memory B and T lymphocytes generated during primary responses that persist and mount accelerated responses upon re-encounter with the same antigen.
content.summary: Memory cells provide durable protection without continuous antigen exposure. They underpin vaccination efficacy and explain why some infections confer lifelong immunity.

framing.relevance: Long-term protection after infection or vaccination.

content.title: Lymphocyte Activation Requirements
content.definition: Dual-signal requirement for full lymphocyte activation involving antigen receptor engagement plus co-stimulatory signals, with MHC molecules presenting processed antigen to T cells.
content.summary: T cells require MHC-presented peptide antigen and co-stimulation to avoid anergy. B cells can be activated by T-dependent or T-independent antigens with distinct outcomes.

framing.relevance: Links antigen presentation to productive adaptive responses.
Explains transplant rejection and anergy in chronic infection.

content.title: Primary vs Secondary Immune Response
content.definition: The slower, lower-magnitude initial adaptive response to a novel antigen compared with the faster, stronger recall response upon re-exposure due to expanded memory lymphocyte populations.
content.summary: Primary responses generate effector cells and memory over days to weeks. Secondary responses mobilize memory B and T cells rapidly, producing higher antibody titers and faster pathogen clearance.

framing.relevance: Explains immunologic memory and the basis for booster vaccinations.
IgM vs IgG switch and vaccine booster rationale.

content.title: Germinal Center Affinity Maturation
content.definition: Somatic hypermutation and selection in germinal centers producing high-affinity antibody variants on memory B cells and plasma cells.
content.summary: B cells compete for antigen and T follicular helper signals in germinal centers. Selected clones expand; others undergo apoptosis.

framing.relevance: High-yield for understanding vaccine response quality.

content.title: Central vs Effector Memory T Cells
content.definition: Memory T-cell subsets distinguished by lymphoid homing versus peripheral tissue surveillance, with different recall kinetics and effector potential.
content.summary: Central memory T cells (CCR7+) circulate through lymph nodes for rapid reactivation. Effector memory T cells (CCR7−) provide immediate tissue surveillance.

framing.title_in_context: Central vs Effector Memory T Cells
framing.relevance: Relevant to chronic viral infection and vaccine design.

content.title: Secondary Response IgM-to-IgG Switch
content.definition: Accelerated class-switch recombination during secondary responses producing predominantly high-affinity IgG with minimal IgM in recall responses.
content.summary: Memory B cells rapidly differentiate into plasma cells upon re-exposure. Class switching and affinity maturation yield qualitatively superior antibody.

framing.relevance: Explains booster serology patterns.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Autoimmunity
content.definition: Loss of self-tolerance leading to immune attack against host antigens through central or peripheral tolerance failure, molecular mimicry, or epitope spreading.
content.summary: Autoimmunity underlies diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and SLE. Genetic susceptibility and environmental triggers interact.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged autoimmunity_basics (biology) and autoimmunity_mechanisms (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: When adaptive immunity targets self antigens.
Pathophysiology of SLE, RA, and other autoimmune diseases on Step 1.
When adaptive immunity targets self antigens.

content.title: Autoantibody Effector Mechanisms
content.definition: Pathogenic mechanisms of self-directed antibodies including type II cytotoxicity, immune complex deposition, and receptor stimulation or blockade.
content.summary: Autoantibodies cause disease through complement-mediated lysis, opsonization, immune complex inflammation, or functional receptor modulation.

framing.relevance: Graves disease (stimulating Ab) vs myasthenia gravis (blocking Ab).

content.title: Representative Autoimmune Diseases
content.definition: Organ-specific and systemic autoimmune conditions including type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus arising from tolerance failure.
content.summary: Autoimmune diseases reflect which self antigens are targeted. Organ-specific diseases attack one tissue type; systemic diseases involve multiple organs and autoantibodies.

framing.relevance: Connects tolerance mechanisms to clinical autoimmune pathology.

content.title: Central and Peripheral Tolerance
content.definition: Mechanisms eliminating or inactivating self-reactive lymphocytes during development in primary lymphoid organs and in peripheral tissues through deletion, anergy, and regulatory T cells.
content.summary: Central tolerance removes strongly autoreactive clones in thymus and bone marrow. Peripheral tolerance restrains low-avidity or tissue-restricted autoreactivity.

framing.relevance: AIRE deficiency and autoimmune polyendocrinopathy.

content.title: Central Tolerance
content.definition: Deletion or inactivation of self-reactive lymphocytes during development in primary lymphoid organs (thymus for T cells, bone marrow for B cells).
content.summary: Negative selection in the thymus eliminates most autoreactive T cells. B cell central tolerance removes self-reactive clones before they enter circulation.

framing.relevance: Primary checkpoint preventing autoreactive lymphocyte maturation.

content.title: Epitope Spreading
content.definition: Progressive broadening of the autoimmune response to additional self-epitopes beyond the initiating antigen during chronic inflammation.
content.summary: Initial tissue damage releases cryptic self-antigens. Spreading amplifies and diversifies autoreactivity, worsening disease course.

framing.relevance: Explains chronicity in MS and type 1 diabetes.

content.title: HLA and Genetic Susceptibility
content.definition: Association between specific HLA alleles and autoimmune disease risk reflecting altered thymic selection and antigen presentation of self-peptides.
content.summary: HLA-DR4 links to RA; HLA-B27 to spondyloarthropathies. Polygenic risk combines with environmental triggers.

framing.relevance: HLA typing in transplant and disease association.

content.title: Molecular Mimicry and Epitope Spreading
content.definition: Autoimmune triggers including pathogen antigens resembling self epitopes (molecular mimicry) and immune responses spreading from initial to additional self epitopes (epitope spreading).
content.summary: Molecular mimicry links infection to autoimmunity when cross-reactive antibodies attack host tissues. Epitope spreading broadens the autoimmune attack over time.

framing.relevance: Environmental triggers that break tolerance.
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis and rheumatic fever.

content.title: Peripheral Tolerance
content.definition: Mechanisms suppressing autoreactive lymphocytes that escape central tolerance, including anergy, regulatory T cells, and activation-induced cell death.
content.summary: Peripheral tolerance provides a second line of defense. Regulatory T cells actively suppress autoreactive responses in tissues and lymphoid organs.

framing.relevance: Backup mechanisms when central tolerance is incomplete.

content.title: Self-Tolerance Overview
content.definition: Mechanisms ensuring lymphocytes do not mount adaptive responses against host antigens, including deletion of self-reactive clones and suppression of autoreactive cells.
content.summary: Self-tolerance prevents autoimmunity under normal conditions. Failure at central or peripheral checkpoints permits immune attack on self tissues.

framing.relevance: Foundation for understanding why autoimmunity is pathological.

content.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis Pathogenesis
content.definition: Chronic symmetric polyarthritis driven by autoantibodies (RF, anti-CCP), T-cell activation, and cytokine-mediated synovial inflammation with pannus formation.
content.summary: Anti-CCP antibodies are highly specific for RA. TNF and IL-6 drive synovitis and joint destruction.

framing.relevance: Biologic therapy targets (TNF inhibitors).

content.title: SLE and Anti-dsDNA Antibodies
content.definition: Systemic lupus erythematosus characterized by anti-dsDNA and anti-nuclear antibodies causing immune complex glomerulonephritis and multisystem inflammation.
content.summary: Anti-dsDNA correlates with disease activity and lupus nephritis. Immune complex deposition in glomeruli drives renal injury.

framing.relevance: Classic Step 1 autoimmune disease.

content.title: Type 1 Diabetes Autoimmunity
content.definition: T-cell-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells with autoantibodies against insulin, GAD65, and IA-2 marking preclinical autoimmune diabetes.
content.summary: Insulitis reflects autoreactive T-cell attack on islets. Progressive beta-cell loss causes insulin deficiency.

framing.relevance: HLA-DR3/DR4 association and islet autoantibodies.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Hypersensitivity Reactions
content.definition: Excessive or misdirected immune responses damaging host tissues through type I (IgE-mediated), type II (antibody-mediated cytotoxic), type III (immune complex), or type IV (T cell-mediated) mechanisms.
content.summary: Hypersensitivity types unify allergy, serum sickness, and contact dermatitis under a mechanistic framework. Imbalance between tolerance and attack drives pathology.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged hypersensitivity_reactions in biology and medicine_preclinical.

framing.relevance: Immune pathology when tolerance fails.
High-yield Step 1 mechanistic classification for allergy and drug reactions.
Immune pathology when tolerance fails.

content.title: Gell and Coombs Classification Overview
content.definition: Framework categorizing excessive immune responses into four types by effector mechanism: IgE-mediated (I), cytotoxic antibody (II), immune complex (III), and delayed T-cell (IV).
content.summary: Type classification predicts timing, mediators, and clinical examples. Each type uses distinct adaptive immune effector pathways.

framing.relevance: High-yield Step 1 mechanistic taxonomy.

content.title: Type I IgE-Mediated Hypersensitivity
content.definition: Immediate hypersensitivity mediated by IgE antibodies binding FcεRI on mast cells and basophils, triggering degranulation and allergic symptoms upon allergen re-exposure.
content.summary: Type I reactions include hay fever, asthma, and anaphylaxis. Sensitization produces allergen-specific IgE; subsequent exposure causes rapid mediator release.

framing.relevance: Most common allergic disease mechanism.
Anaphylaxis and allergic rhinitis.

content.title: Type II Antibody-Mediated Cytotoxicity
content.definition: Hypersensitivity in which IgG or IgM antibodies bind cell-surface antigens, leading to complement-mediated lysis, opsonization, or antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.
content.summary: Type II reactions destroy host cells bearing targeted antigens. Examples include hemolytic transfusion reactions and some drug-induced cytopenias.

framing.relevance: Antibody-driven destruction of host cells.

content.title: Type III Immune Complex Disease
content.definition: Tissue damage from deposition of antigen–antibody complexes in vessel walls and tissues, activating complement and recruiting inflammatory cells.
content.summary: Type III reactions cause serum sickness and Arthus reactions. Soluble immune complexes deposit in joints, kidneys, and skin, provoking inflammation.

framing.relevance: Immune complex deposition as a mechanism of tissue injury.

content.title: Type IV T Cell-Mediated Hypersensitivity
content.definition: Delayed hypersensitivity driven by sensitized T cells releasing cytokines and recruiting macrophages, without antibody involvement.
content.summary: Type IV reactions include contact dermatitis and tuberculin skin tests. Effector T cells mediate tissue damage hours to days after antigen exposure.

framing.relevance: Cell-mediated immune pathology without antibodies.

content.title: Type II (Cytotoxic Antibody) Hypersensitivity
content.definition: Antibody-mediated destruction of cells or matrix via complement activation, opsonization, or antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity against cell-surface antigens.
content.summary: IgG or IgM binds fixed tissue antigens. Complement and phagocytes eliminate targeted cells.

framing.relevance: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia and Goodpasture syndrome.

content.title: Type III (Immune Complex) Hypersensitivity
content.definition: Deposition of antigen–antibody complexes in tissues activating complement and recruiting neutrophils, causing vasculitis and organ inflammation.
content.summary: Complex size and solubility determine deposition sites. Serum sickness and Arthus reaction are prototypical.

framing.relevance: SLE nephritis and serum sickness.

content.title: Type IV (Delayed T-Cell) Hypersensitivity
content.definition: T-cell-mediated tissue injury occurring 24–72 hours after antigen exposure, without antibody involvement, driven by sensitized CD4+ or CD8+ T cells.
content.summary: Delayed-type hypersensitivity involves macrophage activation and cytotoxic T-cell killing. PPD skin test and contact dermatitis are classic examples.

framing.relevance: TB skin test and poison ivy dermatitis.

content.title: Anaphylaxis Mediators and Treatment
content.definition: Systemic type I reaction with rapid hypotension, bronchospasm, and urticaria mediated by histamine and leukotrienes; epinephrine is first-line treatment.
content.summary: Anaphylaxis requires immediate IM epinephrine. Antihistamines and steroids are adjunctive but do not replace epinephrine.

framing.relevance: Emergency management high-yield.

content.title: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (Type II)
content.definition: Warm or cold autoantibodies against RBC surface antigens causing extravascular or intravascular hemolysis as a type II hypersensitivity manifestation.
content.summary: Warm AIHA involves IgG against Rh antigens with splenic sequestration. Cold agglutinin disease involves IgM and complement.

framing.relevance: Direct Coombs test interpretation.

content.title: PPD and TB Skin Test (Type IV)
content.definition: Delayed hypersensitivity reaction to tuberculin protein reflecting prior sensitization by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, read at 48–72 hours as induration.
content.summary: PPD tests cell-mediated immunity, not antibody. False negatives occur in immunosuppression; BCG can cause false positives.

framing.title_in_context: PPD and TB Skin Test (Type IV)
framing.relevance: TB screening and immunocompromise.

content.title: Serum Sickness Mechanism
content.definition: Type III reaction 1–2 weeks after foreign protein exposure when antigen–antibody complexes deposit in joints, kidneys, and vessels causing fever, rash, and arthralgia.
content.summary: Serum sickness follows antitoxin or drug-protein conjugate administration. Complement consumption and neutrophil infiltration drive symptoms.

framing.relevance: Drug-induced serum sickness.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Innate Immunity
content.definition: Immediate, non-specific host defenses including barriers, phagocytes, complement, inflammation, and pattern-recognition receptors detecting pathogen-associated molecular patterns.
content.summary: Innate immunity provides first-line protection before adaptive responses mature. TLRs and complement link recognition to rapid effector functions.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged innate_immunity (biology) and innate_immunity_pattern_recognition (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Evolutionarily ancient immune layer in all multicellular hosts.
Step 1 foundation for sepsis, complement deficiencies, and TLR signaling.
Evolutionarily ancient immune layer in all multicellular hosts.

content.title: Complement System Activation
content.definition: Plasma protein cascade generating C3 convertase through classical, alternative, and lectin pathways to opsonize pathogens and assemble membrane attack complexes.
content.summary: Complement amplifies innate clearance via opsonization, anaphylatoxin release, and direct lysis. Deficiencies predispose to recurrent infections, especially Neisseria.

framing.relevance: C3/C5 deficiency and hereditary angioedema are board-relevant.

content.title: Complement System
content.definition: Cascade of plasma proteins activated by classical, alternative, or lectin pathways to opsonize pathogens, recruit inflammatory cells, and form membrane attack complexes.
content.summary: Complement bridges innate recognition and effector functions. C3 convertase is the central amplification step; terminal components can lyse susceptible microbes.

framing.relevance: Humoral innate effector cascade enhancing pathogen clearance.

content.title: Inflammatory Mediator Release
content.definition: Soluble mediators including cytokines, prostaglandins, bradykinin, and histamine that mediate vasodilation, permeability, pain, and leukocyte recruitment in acute inflammation.
content.summary: Innate activation produces a coordinated mediator profile causing cardinal signs of inflammation. Dysregulated release contributes to sepsis and tissue injury.

framing.relevance: Cytokine storm and NSAID targets connect innate mediators to therapeutics.

content.title: Inflammatory Response
content.definition: Local tissue response to injury or infection characterized by redness, heat, swelling, and pain mediated by cytokines, histamine, prostaglandins, and recruited leukocytes.
content.summary: Inflammation confines infection and initiates repair. Acute inflammation recruits neutrophils; chronic inflammation can cause tissue damage if unresolved.

framing.relevance: Coordinated innate response to tissue injury and infection.

content.title: Pattern-Recognition Receptors
content.definition: Innate immune receptors detecting conserved pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) to initiate inflammatory responses.
content.summary: TLRs, NLRs, and RLRs distinguish self from non-self at the molecular level. PRR engagement triggers cytokine production and phagocyte activation.

framing.relevance: Molecular sensors linking pathogen detection to innate effector functions.
TLR defects and hyperactivation underpin sepsis and autoinflammatory disease.

content.title: Phagocytosis and Opsonization
content.definition: Engulfment and destruction of pathogens by neutrophils and macrophages, enhanced when particles are coated with opsonins such as complement C3b or antibodies.
content.summary: Phagocytes internalize microbes into phagolysosomes for enzymatic killing. Opsonization marks pathogens for efficient recognition and uptake.

framing.relevance: Cellular elimination of pathogens by innate leukocytes.
Chronic granulomatous disease and leukocyte adhesion defects impair this step.

content.title: Physical and Anatomic Barriers
content.definition: First-line innate defenses including intact skin, mucous membranes, cilia, lysozyme, and low gastric pH that prevent pathogen entry.
content.summary: Physical barriers exclude most microbes before immune recognition occurs. Mucociliary clearance and chemical secretions provide additional non-specific protection.

framing.relevance: First line of host defense before cellular immunity.
First-line defense in sepsis risk and mucosal immunity.

content.title: TLR Signaling and MyD88 Pathway
content.definition: Adaptor-dependent signaling downstream of Toll-like receptors, prominently through MyD88, leading to NF-κB and MAPK activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine transcription.
content.summary: MyD88-dependent TLR signaling drives rapid cytokine release in bacterial infection. Alternative adaptors mediate type I interferon responses for endosomal TLRs.

framing.relevance: MyD88 pathway is high-yield for endotoxin shock and immunodeficiency.

content.title: C5a and C3a Anaphylatoxins
content.definition: Complement cleavage fragments C3a and C5a that act as chemotactic and pro-inflammatory mediators promoting mast cell degranulation and neutrophil recruitment.
content.summary: Anaphylatoxins link complement activation to rapid inflammatory cell influx. C5a is among the most potent neutrophil chemoattractants in acute inflammation.

framing.relevance: Connects complement to hypersensitivity and sepsis.

content.title: LPS–TLR4–MD2 Complex (Endotoxin)
content.definition: Gram-negative lipopolysaccharide recognition by the TLR4–MD2–CD14 complex on innate cells triggering systemic inflammatory responses in endotoxemia.
content.summary: Endotoxin is the canonical TLR4 ligand driving septic shock physiology. LPS structure and lipid A moiety determine immunostimulatory potency.

framing.relevance: Gram-negative sepsis mechanism — high-yield Step 1.

content.title: MAC (C5b-9) and Cell Lysis
content.definition: Terminal complement assembly of C5b-9 forming a membrane attack complex that inserts into target membranes and causes osmotic lysis of susceptible microbes.
content.summary: MAC provides direct bactericidal activity especially against Neisseria. Defects in terminal complement components increase risk of recurrent meningococcal infection.

framing.relevance: C5–C9 deficiency and Neisseria susceptibility.

content.title: TLR Location: Cell Surface vs Endosomal
content.definition: Compartmental distribution of Toll-like receptors with cell-surface TLRs detecting microbial surface ligands and endosomal TLRs sensing nucleic acids from internalized pathogens.
content.summary: TLR4 and TLR5 localize to the plasma membrane, whereas TLR3, TLR7, TLR8, and TLR9 signal from endosomes after ligand internalization. Localization determines which PAMPs each TLR detects.

framing.title_in_context: TLR Location: Cell Surface vs Endosomal
framing.relevance: Explains viral nucleic acid sensing vs bacterial cell-wall sensing.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Membrane Potential
content.definition: Voltage difference across a membrane arising from unequal ion distributions and selective permeability, establishing the electrochemical baseline for excitability and transport.
content.summary: Resting membrane potential stores energy for signaling in neurons, muscle, and cardiac tissue. Ion pumps and channel permeability set the equilibrium contributions of each ion species.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Foundation for excitable cell signaling in animals.
Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.
Resting potential and threshold in neurons and skeletal muscle — basis for electrophysiology and pharmacology.
Foundation for excitable cell signaling in animals.

content.title: Electrochemical Driving Force on Ions
content.definition: Net force on an ion combining chemical and electrical gradients, determining direction and magnitude of ion flux when channels open.
content.summary: Driving force = Vm − Eion. Positive driving force on Na+ during rest drives inward current upon channel opening.

framing.relevance: Explains depolarizing vs hyperpolarizing currents.
applications: Na+ inward driving force at rest
K+ outward driving force at rest

content.title: Electrochemical Gradient
content.definition: Combined electrical and chemical driving forces on an ion across the membrane, determining the direction and magnitude of passive ion flux.
content.summary: Ions move down their electrochemical gradients through open channels. The equilibrium potential is the voltage at which electrical and chemical forces balance for a given ion.

framing.relevance: Unified framework for understanding ion movement across membranes.

content.title: Equilibrium vs Steady-State Membrane Potential
content.definition: Resting potential is maintained by active transport despite passive ion leak.
content.summary: True equilibrium would require zero net flux for all ions simultaneously, which does not hold in living cells.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz Resting Potential
content.definition: Weighted combination of ion equilibrium potentials based on relative permeabilities.
content.summary: Resting potential sits near E_K because potassium permeability dominates at rest.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Ion Concentration Gradients
content.definition: Unequal distribution of Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, and other ions across the plasma membrane established by selective permeability and active transport.
content.summary: High intracellular K⁺ and low intracellular Na⁺ create chemical gradients that drive ion movement. These gradients store potential energy for electrical signaling.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Chemical basis for membrane voltage in excitable cells.
Foundation for diuretics and channelopathies.

framing.title_in_context: Ion Concentration Gradients Across the Membrane
framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Na+/K+-ATPase and Resting Potential Maintenance
content.definition: Primary active transport exchanging three Na+ out for two K+ in per ATP hydrolyzed, sustaining ion gradients that establish negative resting membrane potential.
content.summary: The Na+/K+ pump is electrogenic and consumes significant metabolic energy in neurons. Ouabain inhibits the pump, depolarizing cells.

framing.relevance: Digoxin mechanism and hyperkalemia effects.

content.title: Nernst Equilibrium Potential
content.definition: Membrane voltage at which the electrical driving force on an ion exactly balances its chemical gradient, calculated from intra- and extracellular concentrations.
content.summary: Each permeable ion contributes an equilibrium potential. The resting potential reflects weighted influence of multiple ions at partial permeability.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Predicts direction of ion flux during depolarization.
Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Resting Membrane Potential
content.definition: Stable negative voltage across the membrane of unstimulated excitable cells, typically near −70 mV in neurons, maintained by ion gradients and selective permeability.
content.summary: Resting potential represents a steady state, not equilibrium. It provides the baseline from which depolarizing and hyperpolarizing signals are measured.

framing.relevance: Baseline voltage for all electrical signaling in excitable cells.
Baseline for threshold and excitability.

content.title: Selective Membrane Permeability
content.definition: Differential ion flux across the membrane governed by leak channels and gated channels, with potassium permeability dominating at rest in most neurons.
content.summary: Membrane permeability determines which ions contribute most to membrane voltage. At rest, K⁺ leak channels keep the membrane near the potassium equilibrium potential.

framing.relevance: Explains why resting potential is not zero.

content.title: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump
content.definition: Primary active transporter exchanging three Na⁺ out of the cell for two K⁺ in, consuming ATP to maintain ion gradients against electrochemical equilibrium.
content.summary: The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase is essential for long-term gradient maintenance. It counteracts passive leak and restores ions lost during action potentials.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Active transport maintaining resting ion distributions.
Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Depolarizing vs Hyperpolarizing Shifts in Baseline
content.definition: Permeability and extracellular ion changes move resting voltage.
content.summary: Clinical hyperkalemia narrows excitability margins by depolarizing rest.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.title_in_context: Depolarizing vs Hyperpolarizing Shifts in Baseline
framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Relative Permeability and Emphasis on K⁺ at Rest
content.definition: Why resting potential is drawn toward potassium equilibrium.
content.summary: Increasing sodium permeability depolarizes resting baseline toward threshold.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.title_in_context: Relative Permeability and Emphasis on K⁺ at Rest
framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

content.title: Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Voltage Equation
content.definition: Extension of Nernst equation weighting multiple ion permeabilities to predict membrane potential when more than one ion is permeable simultaneously.
content.summary: GHK equation shows resting potential lies between individual equilibrium potentials, closer to the ion with highest relative permeability.

framing.relevance: Quantitative electrophysiology for exams.

content.title: Hypokalemia and Membrane Hyperpolarization
content.definition: Reduced extracellular potassium hyperpolarizes resting membrane potential, decreasing excitability in skeletal muscle and cardiac tissue.
content.summary: Low [K+]o increases K+ equilibrium potential magnitude, making cells harder to depolarize to threshold. ECG changes reflect cardiac effects.

framing.relevance: Electrolyte disorders and ECG correlation.

content.title: Calculating Single-Ion Reversal Potentials
content.definition: Logarithmic Nernst equation with valence and concentration ratio.
content.summary: Practice with E_K and E_Na builds intuition for synaptic driving forces.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged resting_membrane_potential (psych), cell_membrane_resting_potential (medicine_preclinical neuronal), and general excitable-tissue potential. Cardiac membrane potential remains a separate med node (B2).

framing.relevance: Baseline voltage before synaptic input in neurons.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Neurotransmitters and Receptors
content.definition: Chemical messengers released at synapses binding ionotropic or metabotropic receptors to modulate postsynaptic excitability, plasticity, and effector organ function.
content.summary: Major systems include glutamate, GABA, dopamine, serotonin, and acetylcholine. Receptor subtype and location determine pharmacologic and behavioral effects.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Chemical basis of intercellular signaling in nervous systems.
Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.
Basis for autonomic pharmacology, anesthesia, and psychiatric drug classes.
Chemical basis of intercellular signaling in nervous systems.

content.title: Catecholamine and Dopamine Signaling
content.definition: Biosynthesis of dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine from tyrosine with distinct receptor subtypes (D1–D5, α, β) mediating diverse autonomic and CNS effects.
content.summary: Catecholamines act through GPCRs. Dopamine pathways in basal ganglia underlie motor and reward circuitry.

framing.relevance: Parkinson disease and sympathomimetic drugs.

content.title: Cholinergic Signaling
content.definition: Acetylcholine-mediated neurotransmission at nicotinic receptors (ionotropic) in autonomic ganglia and NMJ and muscarinic receptors (GPCR) in parasympathetic effectors.
content.summary: ACh is synthesized from choline and acetyl-CoA. Acetylcholinesterase terminates signaling at synapses.

framing.relevance: Organophosphate poisoning and myasthenia gravis.

content.title: Excitatory vs Inhibitory Neurotransmitters
content.definition: Classification by postsynaptic effect: excitatory transmitters depolarize toward threshold (glutamate, ACh at NMJ), inhibitory transmitters hyperpolarize or shunt (GABA, glycine).
content.summary: Balance of EPSPs and IPSPs determines neuronal output. GABA is the major CNS inhibitory transmitter.

framing.relevance: Seizures (too much excitation) and benzodiazepines.

content.title: Ionotropic Receptors
content.definition: Ligand-gated ion channels that produce rapid postsynaptic potential changes by directly allowing ion flux upon neurotransmitter binding.
content.summary: Ionotropic receptors mediate fast synaptic transmission. Examples include nicotinic ACh receptors and AMPA glutamate receptors.

framing.relevance: Fast postsynaptic responses in chemical synapses.

content.title: Ligand-Gated Ion Channels vs Metabotropic Receptors
content.definition: Ionotropic receptors mediate fast synaptic potentials via direct ion channel opening; metabotropic GPCRs modulate ion channels through second messenger cascades.
content.summary: Nicotinic ACh and GABA-A are ionotropic. Muscarinic ACh and adrenergic receptors are metabotropic with slower, modulatory effects.

framing.title_in_context: Ligand-Gated Ion Channels vs Metabotropic Receptors
framing.relevance: Drug receptor targets for anesthesia and psychiatry.

content.title: Major Neurotransmitter Systems
content.definition: Principal neurotransmitters including glutamate (excitatory), GABA (inhibitory), acetylcholine, dopamine, and serotonin with distinct synthesis pathways and functional roles.
content.summary: Each neurotransmitter system has characteristic anatomical distribution and behavioral correlates. Balance between excitation and inhibition is essential for normal neural function.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Maps chemical messengers to nervous system function.
Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: Metabotropic Receptors
content.definition: G protein-coupled neurotransmitter receptors that initiate intracellular signaling cascades producing slower, modulatory changes in postsynaptic excitability.
content.summary: Metabotropic receptors amplify signals through second messengers. They modulate ion channel activity and gene expression on slower timescales than ionotropic receptors.

framing.relevance: Slow modulatory synaptic signaling via G proteins.

content.title: Reuptake, Enzymatic Degradation, and Clearance
content.definition: Termination of synaptic signals via transporters, degradative enzymes, and diffusion.
content.summary: Reuptake transporters are targets for antidepressants and stimulants.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: Neurotransmitter Synthesis and Release
content.definition: Biosynthesis of neurotransmitters in presynaptic neurons, packaging into synaptic vesicles, and calcium-triggered exocytotic release into the synaptic cleft.
content.summary: Neurotransmitter availability depends on synthesis, vesicle loading, and regulated release. Action potential arrival opens voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels to trigger fusion.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Presynaptic steps converting electrical signals to chemical messengers.
VMAT inhibitors and synthesis enzyme targets.
Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: Ionotropic vs Metabotropic Receptors
content.definition: Ligand-gated ion channels versus G-protein-coupled receptors mediating slower modulatory signaling.
content.summary: Ionotropic receptors produce fast EPSPs/IPSPs; metabotropic receptors alter second messengers and channel phosphorylation.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: EPSPs, IPSPs, and Synaptic Integration
content.definition: Summation of excitatory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs) and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (IPSPs) through temporal and spatial integration at the axon hillock.
content.summary: Multiple synaptic inputs combine algebraically. Net depolarization toward threshold determines whether the postsynaptic neuron fires an action potential.

framing.relevance: How neurons combine multiple synaptic inputs.

content.title: Mesolimbic and Nigrostriatal Dopamine Pathways
content.definition: Distinct dopamine projections supporting reward, movement, and executive function.
content.summary: Hyperdopaminergic mesolimbic signaling is implicated in positive psychotic symptoms.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: GABA-A Receptor and Benzodiazepine Site
content.definition: Chloride-permeable GABA-A receptor with allosteric benzodiazepine binding site that enhances GABA-mediated inhibition.
content.summary: Benzodiazepines increase frequency of Cl− channel opening. Barbiturates increase duration. Both potentiate GABA-A.

framing.relevance: Anxiolytics, sedatives, and overdose management.

content.title: Ionotropic Receptor Kinetics and Conductances
content.definition: Opening, desensitization, and ion selectivity of ligand-gated channels.
content.summary: NMDA receptor magnesium block links channel opening to depolarization.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged neurotransmitters_and_receptors (psychology_neuroscience and medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Maps brain chemistry to behavior and psychopharmacology.

content.title: Nicotinic ACh at the Neuromuscular Junction
content.definition: Acetylcholine binding to nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate causing Na+ influx, end-plate potential, and skeletal muscle action potential initiation.
content.summary: NMJ nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated Na+/K+ channels. Curare and succinylcholine act at this receptor.

framing.title_in_context: Nicotinic ACh at the Neuromuscular Junction
framing.relevance: Neuromuscular blockers for anesthesia.

content.title: Parkinson Disease and Dopamine Deficiency
content.definition: Degeneration of substantia nigra dopaminergic neurons reducing striatal dopamine, causing bradykinesia, rigidity, and resting tremor.
content.summary: L-DOPA crosses the blood-brain barrier and replenishes dopamine. Dopamine itself cannot cross the BBB.

framing.relevance: L-DOPA/carbidopa therapy rationale.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Prokaryotic Cell Structure
content.definition: Unicellular organisms lacking a nucleus, with circular DNA, ribosomes, cell wall, and often flagella or pili defining bacterial and archaeal architecture.
content.summary: Prokaryotic simplicity enables rapid replication. Cell envelope composition distinguishes gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged prokaryotic_cell_structure (biology) and bacterial_cell_structure (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Structural and evolutionary framing across bacterial and archaeal domains.
Gram staining, virulence factors, and antibiotic targets on Step 1.
Structural and evolutionary framing across bacterial and archaeal domains.

content.title: Flagella and Pili
content.definition: External prokaryotic structures where flagella provide motility via rotary motion and pili mediate attachment and conjugative DNA transfer.
content.summary: Flagellar rotation is powered by proton motive force. Sex pili connect donor and recipient cells during conjugation.

framing.relevance: Motility and genetic exchange structures in bacteria.

content.title: Bacterial Cell Envelope
content.definition: Layered structure comprising plasma membrane, peptidoglycan cell wall, and in gram-negative bacteria an outer membrane with lipopolysaccharide.
content.summary: The cell envelope provides shape, osmotic protection, and a permeability barrier. Gram stain distinguishes thick peptidoglycan (gram-positive) from thin peptidoglycan plus outer membrane (gram-negative).

framing.relevance: Structural basis for gram staining and antibiotic targets.

content.title: Bacterial Flagella and Pili
content.definition: External appendages for motility (flagella) and adherence/conjugation (pili/fimbriae) extending from the bacterial surface.
content.summary: Flagella rotate for chemotaxis. Type IV pili mediate twitching motility; sex pili transfer plasmids during conjugation.

framing.relevance: UTI adherence via fimbriae.

content.title: Bacterial Ribosomes and Antibiotic Targets
content.definition: 70S prokaryotic ribosomes (30S and 50S subunits) differing from 80S eukaryotic ribosomes, enabling selective inhibition by aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, macrolides, and chloramphenicol.
content.summary: Antibiotics exploit structural differences in ribosomal RNA and proteins. Misuse promotes resistance.

framing.relevance: Protein synthesis inhibitor mechanisms.

content.title: Gram-Positive vs Gram-Negative Classification
content.definition: Differential staining based on peptidoglycan thickness and outer membrane presence, dividing bacteria into gram-positive (purple) and gram-negative (pink) groups.
content.summary: Gram classification reflects fundamental envelope architecture. It predicts antibiotic susceptibility and informs clinical microbiology.

framing.relevance: Key diagnostic classification in microbiology.

content.title: Gram Stain and Cell Wall Properties
content.definition: Differential staining based on peptidoglycan thickness and lipid content distinguishing gram-positive (purple) from gram-negative (pink) bacteria.
content.summary: Crystal violet retention in thick peptidoglycan defines gram-positive organisms. Outer membrane of gram-negatives prevents dye retention after alcohol decolorization.

framing.title_in_context: Gram Stain and Cell Wall Properties
framing.relevance: Initial microbiology identification step.

content.title: Plasmids and Horizontal Gene Transfer
content.definition: Extrachromosomal circular DNA carrying resistance genes and virulence factors, transferred between bacteria via conjugation, transformation, or transduction.
content.summary: Horizontal transfer accelerates antibiotic resistance spread. R-plasmids confer multi-drug resistance.

framing.relevance: MRSA and resistance epidemiology.

content.title: Prokaryotic Cell Envelope (Wall and Membrane)
content.definition: Peptidoglycan cell wall surrounding the plasma membrane in bacteria, with gram-positive thick single layer and gram-negative thin wall plus outer membrane.
content.summary: The envelope provides osmotic protection and antibiotic targets. Lysozyme cleaves peptidoglycan; beta-lactams inhibit cross-linking.

framing.relevance: Gram stain and antibiotic selectivity.

content.title: Prokaryotic Genome and Plasmids
content.definition: Circular double-stranded DNA chromosome in the nucleoid region plus optional small extrachromosomal plasmids carrying accessory genes such as antibiotic resistance.
content.summary: Prokaryotic genomes are compact with little noncoding DNA. Plasmids enable horizontal gene transfer and rapid adaptation.

framing.relevance: Genetic organization enabling rapid replication and adaptation.

content.title: Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Organization
content.definition: Fundamental differences between prokaryotic cells lacking a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles and eukaryotic cells with compartmentalized organelles.
content.summary: Prokaryotes include bacteria and archaea. Their simpler architecture enables rapid growth and genetic exchange but limits compartmental specialization.

framing.relevance: Evolutionary and structural context for microbial cells.

content.title: Beta-Lactam Antibiotics and Cell Wall Synthesis
content.definition: Beta-lactam antibiotics inhibit transpeptidases (penicillin-binding proteins) preventing peptidoglycan cross-linking and causing bacterial lysis.
content.summary: Bactericidal effect is greatest on actively dividing bacteria with intact cell wall synthesis. Resistance via beta-lactamase enzymes.

framing.title_in_context: Beta-Lactam Antibiotics and Cell Wall Synthesis
framing.relevance: Penicillin mechanism and resistance.

content.title: Gram-Negative Outer Membrane and LPS
content.definition: Outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria containing lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in the outer leaflet, acting as endotoxin and permeability barrier.
content.summary: LPS lipid A triggers TLR4-mediated inflammation. Porins allow passage of small hydrophilic molecules.

framing.relevance: Endotoxin and septic shock.

generation_parameters.audience_description: Undergraduate biology student. Assumed prior knowledge includes general biology and introductory cell biology.

content.title: Synaptic Transmission
content.definition: Chemical communication at synapses where presynaptic neurotransmitter release activates postsynaptic receptors, producing graded potentials integrated by target cells.
content.summary: Synapses convert presynaptic electrical activity into chemical signals. Structure, release probability, and receptor density determine synaptic strength and plasticity.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Links cellular excitability to circuit-level integration.
Foundation of neural network computation and learning.
Target for reuptake inhibitors, local anesthetics, and neuromuscular blockers.
Links cellular excitability to circuit-level integration.

content.title: Calcium-Triggered Vesicle Fusion
content.definition: Voltage-gated calcium influx coupling action potentials to synaptic vesicle exocytosis.
content.summary: Calcium sensors trigger fusion of docked vesicles, releasing neurotransmitter into the cleft.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Foundation of neural network computation and learning.

content.title: Chemical Synapse Structure
content.definition: Specialized junction with presynaptic terminal containing vesicles, synaptic cleft, and postsynaptic membrane bearing neurotransmitter receptors.
content.summary: Synaptic structure determines transmission direction and specificity. The synaptic cleft separates pre- and postsynaptic membranes, preventing direct electrical coupling.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Anatomical basis for directed chemical communication.
Foundation of neural network computation and learning.

content.title: Excitatory vs Inhibitory Synapses
content.definition: Synaptic classification based on whether neurotransmitter–receptor binding depolarizes (excitatory) or hyperpolarizes (inhibitory) the postsynaptic membrane.
content.summary: Excitatory synapses bring membrane potential toward threshold; inhibitory synapses move it away. Neural circuits balance both to regulate output.

framing.relevance: Determines net effect of synaptic input on postsynaptic firing.

content.title: Postsynaptic Potentials
content.definition: Graded, local changes in postsynaptic membrane potential produced by neurotransmitter binding to receptors, decaying with distance from the synapse.
content.summary: Postsynaptic potentials are subthreshold and decremental. Their amplitude depends on the number of vesicles released and receptor sensitivity.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Local voltage changes that integrate toward action potential threshold.
Neural integration and anesthesia effects.
Foundation of neural network computation and learning.

content.title: Presynaptic Vesicle Release Machinery
content.definition: Calcium-triggered fusion of synaptic vesicles with the presynaptic membrane via SNARE complex proteins releasing neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft.
content.summary: Action potential invasion opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels. Ca2+ binds synaptotagmin, triggering SNARE-mediated exocytosis.

framing.relevance: Botulinum toxin SNARE cleavage.

content.title: Short-Term Synaptic Plasticity
content.definition: Facilitation, depression, and post-tetanic potentiation modifying synaptic strength over seconds to minutes.
content.summary: Presynaptic vesicle depletion and calcium buildup tune synaptic gain during burst firing.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Foundation of neural network computation and learning.

content.title: Synaptic Cleft Clearance Mechanisms
content.definition: Reuptake transporters and enzymatic degradation terminating synaptic signaling by removing neurotransmitter from the cleft.
content.summary: SSRIs block serotonin reuptake. Acetylcholinesterase hydrolyzes ACh at the NMJ.

knowledge_graph._shared_concept_note: Merged synapse_structure (biology), synaptic_transmission (psych), synaptic_transmission_physiology (medicine_preclinical).

framing.relevance: Reuptake inhibitor pharmacology.
Foundation of neural network computation and learning.
